title: Model Withdrawal / Recall Execution Audit (Art. 12/72)

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'EU AI Act' → 'Art.13'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that EU AI Act Art.13 (Transparency and information) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.21.23: Model Withdrawal / Recall Execution Audit (Art. 12/72).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.21.23' running on index=ai_edge sourcetype=ai:recall:ack — recall_id, node_id, ack_time, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=ai_edge sourcetype="ai:recall:ack" earliest=-30d
| stats min(ack_time) as first_ack, dc(node_id) as nodes_acked by recall_id
| join type=left recall_id [
    search index=ai_edge sourcetype="ai:recall:target" earliest=-30d
    | stats dc(node_id) as nodes_total by recall_id
  ]
| eval pct=round(100*nodes_acked/nodes_total,2)
| where pct < 100
| table recall_id, nodes_acked, nodes_total, pct, first_ack
description: Verifies recall_command acknowledged by each edge node within SLA for model withdrawal evidence.
value: Verifies recall_command acknowledged by each edge node within SLA for model withdrawal evidence.
implementation: (1) Maintain authoritative node inventory; (2) alert pct < 100 after 4h; (3) ITSI episode per recall_id; (4) store signed ack externally.
visualization: Bar chart (pct by recall_id), Table (lagging nodes), Single value (min pct)
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We check recall_command acknowledged by each edge node within service promises for model withdrawal evidence — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ai_edge` `sourcetype=ai:recall:ack` — recall_id, node_id, ack_time.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain authoritative node inventory; (2) alert pct < 100 after 4h; (3) ITSI episode per recall_id; (4) store signed ack externally.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ai_edge sourcetype="ai:recall:ack" earliest=-30d
| stats min(ack_time) as first_ack, dc(node_id) as nodes_acked by recall_id
| join type=left recall_id [
    search index=ai_edge sourcetype="ai:recall:target" earliest=-30d
    | stats dc(node_id) as nodes_total by recall_id
  ]
| eval pct=round(100*nodes_acked/nodes_total,2)
| where pct < 100
| table recall_id, nodes_acked, nodes_total, pct, first_ack
```

Understanding this SPL

**Model Withdrawal / Recall Execution Audit (Art. 12/72) (EU AI Act)** — Verifies recall_command acknowledged by each edge node within SLA for model withdrawal evidence.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ai_edge` `sourcetype=ai:recall:ack` — recall_id, node_id, ack_time. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ai_edge; **sourcetype**: ai:recall:ack. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ai_edge, sourcetype="ai:recall:ack", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by recall_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **pct** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where pct < 100` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Model Withdrawal / Recall Execution Audit (Art. 12/72) (EU AI Act)**): table recall_id, nodes_acked, nodes_total, pct, first_ack


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (pct by recall_id), Table (lagging nodes), Single value (min pct)
